attributes.label: Scope alignment to actual problem
attributes.description: Whether the document solves the right problem. The actual task is: help a corporate communications team structure press releases and blog posts so they perform well in consumer AI search products (Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT, Perplexity). A document that instead explains LLM architecture for a technical audience, or optimizes for enterprise RAG systems, or provides only principles without operational guidance, is solving a different problem regardless of how well it does so.

fergus_v1.hover: Solves a different problem: 'teach a technical audience about LLM architecture.' The actual problem — 'help a comms team structure press releases for AI search' — requires practical, section-level guidance that this document completely omits.
fergus_v1.detail: Fergus v1 reads like a primer written for someone who already understands communications and needs to learn about LLMs. The actual audience has the inverse need — they already know how to write and need to know what to change. The document's scope (LLM architecture, RAG pipeline, semantic search concepts) is adjacent to the problem but doesn't address it. A comms professional who reads it will understand embeddings slightly better but will have no idea how to restructure their next announcement. The enterprise RAG framing adds a second scope error: even the technical explanation is aimed at the wrong system.
fergus_v1.example_quote: Version control: Maintain clear versioning to avoid outdated content in RAG systems. Access controls: Ensure sensitive information is appropriately restricted.
fergus_v1.composite_comparison: The Ideal Composite correctly scopes to the actual problem: helping a comms team structure owned content for consumer AI search products. Its educational section is in service of the practical guidance, not a standalone technical primer.
fergus_v1.composite_quote: When we publish content on our corporate blog or newsroom — whether it's a product announcement, a seasonal campaign, or an evergreen explainer — we are now writing for three audiences at once.

client_orig.hover: Correctly scoped to the actual problem: changing how a comms team writes press releases. The three-audience framework and section-by-section template are exactly what the audience needs. Loses points for omitting the technical publishing and measurement layers.
client_orig.detail: The Client Original is correctly scoped to the right problem and the right audience. Its three-audience framework, section-by-section template, and worked examples directly address the task of helping communications professionals restructure their announcements. The deductions are for missing the technical publishing layer (crawl access, canonical URLs, structured data) and the measurement layer (how to know if the optimization is working) — both of which are necessary for a complete solution to the stated problem.
client_orig.example_quote: How we structure and what we think about when writing external announcements and blogs must change. That does not mean writing like a robot with no emotion to feed the AI machine.
client_orig.composite_comparison: The Ideal Composite preserves the Client Original's correct scoping and adds the missing technical and measurement layers.
client_orig.composite_quote: This guide has two goals. First, provide the communications team with a foundational understanding of how AI systems actually discover, process, and use our content. Second, establish practical best practices for structure and formatting that serve all three audiences.

fergus_v2.hover: Better scoped than v1 — restores the full template and worked examples. But still frames the technical foundation through enterprise RAG rather than consumer AI search, and misses the technical publishing and measurement layers.
fergus_v2.detail: Fergus v2 recovers the correct scope from the Client Original: the full announcement template, section-by-section guidance, and worked examples all address the right problem. However, the technical foundation sections still frame the mechanism through enterprise RAG rather than consumer AI search, which means the 'why' behind the guidance points at the wrong system. The document also continues to omit the technical publishing and measurement layers that are part of a complete solution.
fergus_v2.example_quote: Your published content is crawled by search engines and AI platforms. Text is processed and converted into embeddings. These embeddings are stored in vector databases.
fergus_v2.composite_comparison: The Ideal Composite achieves full scope alignment by combining the correct educational model (two paths to consumer AI), the full editorial template, the technical publishing layer, and the measurement framework.
fergus_v2.composite_quote: For this path, our content needs to do two things: rank in web search so it gets retrieved in the first place, and be easy to extract clear facts from so the AI uses our content accurately in its response.

opus_4_6.hover: Solves half the problem brilliantly: the educational foundation. But omits the other half — the operational guidance that actually changes behavior. The two-path model is the right framework; the document just doesn't build the house on top of it.
opus_4_6.detail: The Opus 4.6 rewrite correctly identifies the problem space (how AI interacts with corporate content) and provides the right educational foundation (two-path model). But it stops before solving the full problem. The stated task is to help a comms team structure press releases for AI search — which requires templates, formatting patterns, worked examples, and checklists. The Opus 4.6 rewrite provides principles and explanations but no operational guidance at the section level. It is the right foundation for the right guide, but it's only the foundation.
opus_4_6.example_quote: Use clear structural formatting. Headlines, subheadings, and 'Key Highlights' sections help AI systems parse content and identify the most important information.
opus_4_6.composite_comparison: The Ideal Composite uses the Opus 4.6 foundation and builds the full operational guide on top of it — template, examples, checklists, technical publishing, measurement, and retail handoff.
opus_4_6.composite_quote: Use the 'Label: Value' structure — bold the label before the colon, follow with specific facts, one idea per bullet. Aim for 5–8 bullets.

gpt_5_4.hover: The best scope alignment of any document. Correctly identifies the problem (consumer AI search), the audience (comms team), and the full solution (editorial + technical + measurement + retail). Only gap is sparse examples.
gpt_5_4.detail: The GPT 5.4 rewrite is the only document that fully aligns to the problem at every layer. It correctly identifies: the mechanism (consumer AI search via web crawling, not enterprise RAG), the audience (comms professionals who need to change their writing), the editorial solution (announcement template with section-by-section guidance), the technical solution (publishing checklist for crawl access, canonical URLs, structured data), the measurement solution (operating rhythm with platform-specific tracking), and the domain solution (retail commerce handoff). Its only scope gap is the sparseness of worked examples within the editorial layer.
gpt_5_4.example_quote: For a retail brand, AI visibility is not only a newsroom issue. Users often move from the announcement to a commerce question.
gpt_5_4.composite_comparison: The Ideal Composite matches GPT 5.4's full scope alignment and fills the example gap with demonstrations from the Client Original and Fergus v2.
gpt_5_4.composite_quote: Pair each announcement with the next authoritative destination. A sale announcement should link to the live sale page. A product launch should link to the canonical product page.

attributes.edge: GPT 5.4 — solves the right problem most completely
